title: Public Health Analytics Playbook
text:
Welcome
The Public Health Analytics Playbook is a collection of data-driven analyses exploring real public health issues. Each chapter takes on a specific topic, pulls data from public sources, walks through the epidemiology, and provides reproducible R and Python code you can adapt for your own work.
This is equal parts curiosity project and practical reference. The analyses are grounded in published data and official statistics. The code is designed to be copied, modified, and reused.

title: Public Health Analytics Playbook
text:
0.1 What This Is
Each chapter in the playbook follows a consistent pattern:

The issue: What is the public health problem, and why does it matter?
The data: Where does the evidence come from, and how do we access it programmatically?
The analysis: Reproducible code (R and/or Python) that explores the data, with narrative interpretation
The visualizations: Charts, maps, and tables that make patterns visible

Some chapters may compare the same issue across jurisdictions (U.S. states, countries, tribal nations). Others may go deep on a single geography. The scope is flexible and issue-driven.

title: Public Health Analytics Playbook
text:
0.2 Who This Is For

Epidemiologists and public health analysts looking for reproducible examples they can adapt
Data scientists curious about public health data sources, APIs, and visualization
Public health students seeking applied analytics beyond textbook exercises
Anyone who wants to understand what the data actually shows about the health issues that shape our communities

title: Public Health Analytics Playbook
text:
0.3 Companion Projects
This playbook is part of a suite of Intersect Collaborations resources:

Public Health Automation Clinic: Practical automation solutions for public health workflows
Bridgeframe Toolkit: Bridging business analysis and public health terminology and practice

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3 The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
The United States stands alone among high-income nations in the scale of its overdose crisis. Drug overdoses claimed more than 100,000 lives for a third consecutive year in 2023, representing over a 50 percent increase since 2019 (Commonwealth Fund, 2025b). With an overdose rate of 324 deaths per million people (nearly 108,000 deaths in 2022), the U.S. has by far the highest overdose death rate in the world. While Scotland and Canada, the next highest-ranked countries, saw declines from 2021 to 2022, rates in the U.S. have remained persistently elevated (Commonwealth Fund, 2025b).
According to the CDC National Center for Health Statistics, the three leading causes of death in the U.S. are heart disease (680,981 deaths), cancer (613,352), and unintentional injuries (222,698), with drug poisonings dominating the latter category (National Center for Health Statistics, 2024b). Drug overdose has become a leading cause of injury mortality, with opioids driving an increasing share of these deaths since 2009 and accounting for over 70% of all overdose fatalities (National Center for Health Statistics, 2024a). The growing involvement of stimulants such as cocaine and methamphetamine alongside opioids has further complicated the crisis, transforming what began as a prescription opioid problem into a polysubstance mass-casualty event.
No peer nation experiences anything comparable. The Commonwealth Fund 2025 Scorecard on State Health System Performance documents that premature, avoidable deaths are increasing in the United States while declining in comparable high-income countries, with drug overdose a major contributor to this divergence (Commonwealth Fund, 2025a). The rate of avoidable mortality in West Virginia is more than twice the rate in Massachusetts, and wide racial disparities in premature deaths are the norm in most states (Commonwealth Fund, 2025a). While overdose deaths fell slightly between 2022 and 2023 in 37 states, the total remains above 100,000 annually, and rates spiked by 25% or more in Nevada, Oregon, Washington, and Alaska (Commonwealth Fund, 2025a).
This chapter explores what the data shows, where the crisis hits hardest, and how to access and analyze the underlying datasets and research literature programmatically.
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.1 Why This Chapter
My path to this topic stems from my educational background and professional experience. I studied psychology and counseling in South Africa, where I worked as a supportive counselor at a residential recovery center for people with substance use disorders. That experience gave me a ground-level understanding of addiction, recovery, and the human cost of substance-related harm.
When I moved to the United States, I helped with the development of COVID-19 contact tracing systems before joining a local health department as an epidemiologist, where my responsibilities included drug overdose mortality monitoring and surveillance. I worked with county-level data coming from different jurisdictions, most of which was not programmatically accessible. After working at the health department, I continued at the CDC on COVID-19 response work and then antimicrobial resistance (AMR) response, but the opioid epidemic never left my peripheral vision.
Now, with more capacity to investigate, I am returning to the overdose landscape with fresh analytical tools and a broader perspective. As the published literature documents, the scale of drug overdose mortality in the United States is without parallel among high-income nations (Commonwealth Fund, 2025a). This chapter is my attempt to understand the current state of the crisis: to see what data is available, to assess the impact and scale, to trace where funding is flowing, to examine what interventions are being tried, and to survey the research being conducted in the field.
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.2 The Scale of the Problem
Drug overdose deaths in the United States have followed a steep, accelerating trajectory over the past 25 years. Approximately 105,000 people died from drug overdose in 2023, with nearly 80,000 (76%) of those deaths involving opioids. The number of opioid overdose deaths in 2023 was nearly ten times the number in 1999, though 2023 marked the first annual decline since 2018 (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025). From 1999 to 2023, approximately 806,000 people died from an opioid overdose in the United States (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025).
The CDC describes three distinct waves of opioid overdose deaths (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025):

Wave 1 (1990s): Increased prescribing of opioids drove a rise in overdose deaths involving prescription opioids (natural and semi-synthetic opioids and methadone) starting around 1999, though these have declined in recent years
Wave 2 (2010): Rapid increases in overdose deaths involving heroin began in 2010, though heroin overdose deaths have also been declining in recent years
Wave 3 (2013 onward): Substantial increases in overdose deaths involving synthetic opioids, particularly illegally manufactured fentanyls (IMFs), which have largely saturated the illegal drug supply

The third wave remains the dominant driver. IMFs are found in powder form or pressed into counterfeit pills and frequently contain other drugs, including non-opioid sedatives such as xylazine (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025). From 2022 to 2023, the rate of overdose deaths involving synthetic opioids decreased approximately 2%, heroin deaths decreased roughly 33%, and prescription opioid deaths decreased nearly 12% (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025). Despite these declines, in 2023, nearly 47% of drug overdose deaths involved both opioids and stimulants, underscoring the polysubstance nature of the current crisis (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2025). Data from the SUDORS Dashboard further confirms this pattern: in 2024, 73.4% of overdose deaths involved at least one opioid and 65.1% involved at least one stimulant (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024c). Critically, 67.5% of overdose deaths had at least one potential opportunity for intervention, and in 2022, 77% of adults with substance use disorder were not receiving treatment (Commonwealth Fund, 2025a).
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.3 Data Sources
The primary datasets for analyzing drug overdose mortality in the United States are:

3.3.1 CDC WONDER
CDC WONDER (Wide-ranging ONline Data for Epidemiologic Research) is the premier federal system for querying mortality data from the National Vital Statistics System. It provides:

Mortality counts, crude rates, and age-adjusted rates
Stratification by geography (national, state, county), demographics, urbanization, and ICD-10 codes
Multiple Cause of Death files that capture all substances listed on the death certificate







WarningData Suppression



CDC WONDER suppresses counts below 10 deaths in any given cell to prevent identification. Analyses of small populations or rare drug categories at the county level will encounter this limitation.




3.3.2 CDC Open Data (WONDER API and Socrata)
CDC publishes several overdose-specific datasets on its open data portal at data.cdc.gov, including provisional drug overdose death counts updated monthly. These datasets are Socrata-hosted and accessible via API.


3.3.3 State and Local Portals
Many states and local jurisdictions maintain their own open data portals with overdose-related datasets, often including more granular geographic or toxicological detail than federal sources. These vary in format, update frequency, and accessibility, but are worth exploring for jurisdiction-specific analyses.
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.4 Accessing the Data with R

3.4.1 VSRR Provisional Drug Overdose Death Counts
The primary dataset used in this chapter is the VSRR Provisional Drug Overdose Death Counts (dataset ID: xkb8-kh2a), published by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) as part of the Vital Statistics Rapid Release (VSRR) program (Ahmad et al., 2026). This dataset presents provisional counts of drug overdose deaths based on the current flow of mortality data in the National Vital Statistics System (NVSS). It is updated monthly, with data available within four months after the date of death.
Provisional counts may not include all deaths that occurred during a given period because drug overdose deaths often require lengthy investigation, including toxicology testing. Death certificates may initially be filed with manner of death “pending investigation” or with a preliminary cause of death. To address this undercount, NCHS developed statistical methods to generate predicted provisional counts that adjust for reporting delays based on historical completeness patterns (Ahmad et al., 2026). Drug overdose deaths are identified using ICD-10 underlying cause-of-death codes: X40–X44 (unintentional), X60–X64 (suicide), X85 (homicide), and Y10–Y14 (undetermined intent).
The dataset reports 12-month ending counts, meaning each row represents the total number of deaths occurring in the 12-month period ending in the month indicated. For example, a row for June 2024 captures deaths from July 2023 through June 2024. This rolling window smooths out seasonal variation and provides a stable trend measure. The indicator field specifies which outcome is being counted: total drug overdose deaths, or deaths involving a specific drug or drug class, identified by ICD-10 multiple cause-of-death codes:

Heroin (T40.1)
Natural opioid analgesics including morphine, codeine, oxycodone, hydrocodone (T40.2)
Methadone (T40.3)
Synthetic opioids other than methadone, including fentanyl and tramadol (T40.4)
Cocaine (T40.5)
Psychostimulants with abuse potential, including methamphetamine (T43.6)

Because a single death may involve multiple substances, it can appear in more than one drug category. For example, a death involving both heroin and fentanyl would be counted under both heroin and synthetic opioids other than methadone. For full column definitions and metadata, see the dataset documentation.






WarningInterpreting Provisional Data



Provisional counts are often lower than final counts due to reporting delays. The predicted_value column provides a corrected estimate but may not fully account for unusually long delays in some jurisdictions. Comparisons of drug-specific death counts across states should be made cautiously because the percentage of death certificates with specific drugs recorded varies by jurisdiction. For definitive counts, use the final mortality data published annually through CDC WONDER.


The following code demonstrates how to pull this dataset from CDC’s Socrata-hosted open data portal.


Code
# CDC provisional drug overdose death counts (Socrata endpoint)
# Dataset: VSRR Provisional Drug Overdose Death Counts
# Source: https://data.cdc.gov/NCHS/VSRR-Provisional-Drug-Overdose-Death-Counts/xkb8-kh2a

cdc_url <- "https://data.cdc.gov/resource/xkb8-kh2a.json"

# Query: US + all states, total drug overdose deaths only
# The Socrata API caps at 50,000 rows per request
response <- request(cdc_url) |>
  req_url_query(
    `$where`  = "indicator = 'Number of Drug Overdose Deaths'",
    `$limit`  = 50000,
    `$order`  = "year ASC, month ASC"
  ) |>
  req_perform()

overdose_raw <- response |>
  resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE)


The query returned 6966 rows covering 54 jurisdictions.






TipReproducibility



The Socrata API does not require authentication for public datasets. The dataset identifier xkb8-kh2a is stable. You can also download the full dataset as CSV from the dataset page. For full methodology, including the statistical models used to generate predicted counts and the data quality thresholds applied to each jurisdiction, see the Technical Notes (Ahmad et al., 2026).



3.4.1.1 Interactive Time Series: Drug Overdose Deaths by Jurisdiction
The plot below shows 12-month ending provisional drug overdose death counts for the United States and every reporting state. Use the legend to toggle individual jurisdictions on or off, hover over data points for exact counts, and use the range slider to zoom into specific time periods.


Code
# Month lookup for proper date ordering
month_lookup <- c(
  "January" = 1, "February" = 2, "March" = 3, "April" = 4,
  "May" = 5, "June" = 6, "July" = 7, "August" = 8,
  "September" = 9, "October" = 10, "November" = 11, "December" = 12
)

overdose_ts <- overdose_raw |>
  mutate(
    data_value     = as.numeric(data_value),
    predicted_value = as.numeric(predicted_value),
    year_num       = as.integer(year),
    month_num      = month_lookup[month],
    date           = as.Date(paste(year_num, month_num, "01", sep = "-"))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(data_value)) |>
  arrange(state_name, date)

# Separate United States (always visible) from states (hidden by default)
us_data    <- overdose_ts |> filter(state_name == "United States")
state_data <- overdose_ts |> filter(state_name != "United States")

# Get sorted list of state names
state_names <- sort(unique(state_data$state_name))

# Build the plotly figure
p <- plot_ly() |>
  # United States trend line (bold, always visible)
  add_trace(
    data = us_data,
    x = ~date, y = ~data_value,
    type = "scatter", mode = "lines",
    name = "United States",
    line = list(color = "#2494f7", width = 3),
    hovertemplate = paste(
      "<b>United States</b><br>",
      "Period ending: %{x|%B %Y}<br>",
      "Reported deaths: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
    )
  )

# Add each state as a separate trace (hidden by default via legendgroup)
for (st in state_names) {
  st_df <- state_data |> filter(state_name == st)
  p <- p |>
    add_trace(
      data = st_df,
      x = ~date, y = ~data_value,
      type = "scatter", mode = "lines",
      name = st,
      visible = "legendonly",
      hovertemplate = paste0(
        "<b>", st, "</b><br>",
        "Period ending: %{x|%B %Y}<br>",
        "Reported deaths: %{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"
      )
    )
}

p <- p |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "12-Month Ending Provisional Drug Overdose Deaths by Jurisdiction",
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title = "12-Month Period Ending",
      rangeslider = list(visible = TRUE),
      type = "date"
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Deaths (12-month ending count)",
      separatethousands = TRUE
    ),
    legend = list(
      orientation = "v",
      x = 1.02, y = 1,
      font = list(size = 10)
    ),
    hovermode = "x unified",
    margin = list(r = 150)
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

p








3.4.1.2 States with the Highest Overdose Deaths
The table below shows the five states with the highest reported drug overdose death counts in the most recent 12-month period available. For the full filterable dataset with all states and time periods, use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer.


Code
overdose_raw |>
  mutate(
    data_value = as.numeric(data_value),
    predicted_value = as.numeric(predicted_value)
  ) |>
  filter(state_name != "United States", !is.na(data_value)) |>
  arrange(desc(year), desc(match(month, month.name))) |>
  distinct(state_name, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  arrange(desc(data_value)) |>
  head(5) |>
  select(
    State = state_name, Year = year, Month = month,
    `Reported Deaths` = data_value, `Predicted Deaths` = predicted_value
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format.args = list(big.mark = ","),
    caption = "Top 5 States by Reported Drug Overdose Deaths (Latest 12-Month Period)"
  )

crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.5 What the Interventions Look Like
Public health responses to the overdose crisis operate at multiple levels:
Harm reduction: Naloxone, a life-saving medication that can reverse opioid overdose, is now available over the counter in all 50 states (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024b). Access can be further expanded through standing orders at pharmacies, distribution by community-based organizations and law enforcement, and training for emergency medical service staff. However, only 1 naloxone prescription is dispensed for every 70 high-dose opioid prescriptions, and rural counties are nearly 3 times more likely to have low dispensing rates than metropolitan counties (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024b). Syringe services programs (SSPs) are community-based prevention programs that provide sterile syringes, testing and vaccination for infectious diseases, linkage to substance use disorder treatment, and naloxone distribution (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024d). Nearly 30 years of research shows that comprehensive SSPs are safe, effective, and cost-saving; they are associated with an estimated 50% reduction in HIV and hepatitis C incidence and do not increase illegal drug use or crime (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024d).
Treatment access: Medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD), particularly buprenorphine and methadone, substantially reduce overdose-related and overall mortality (Dowell et al., 2024). Yet in 2022, among the estimated 3.7% of U.S. adults (9.4 million people) who needed OUD treatment, only 25% received MOUD; most either did not perceive a need for treatment (43%) or received treatment that did not include medications (30%) (Dowell et al., 2024). Racial disparities persist: higher percentages of White adults received any OUD treatment compared with Black or Hispanic adults (Dowell et al., 2024). The 2022 CDC Clinical Practice Guideline provides 12 evidence-based recommendations for clinicians on prescribing opioids and managing pain, and since 2023, a waiver is no longer required to prescribe buprenorphine for OUD (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024a). Federal funding through State Opioid Response (SOR) grants ($1.48 billion in FY2025) and Tribal Opioid Response (TOR) grants (~$63 million) supports treatment, prevention, naloxone distribution, and recovery services nationwide (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, 2025). Since the SOR program began in 2018, nearly 1.3 million people have received treatment services (including over 650,000 who received MOUD), more than 10 million opioid overdose reversal kits have been distributed, and opioid reversal medications have been used to reverse more than 550,000 overdoses (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, 2025).
Surveillance: Enhanced death investigation through SUDORS (State Unintentional Drug Overdose Reporting System) provides toxicological detail beyond what death certificates capture, revealing which specific substances are involved (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2024c).
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.6 Federal Funding
Opioid response funding flows through several federal mechanisms.

3.6.1 USAspending
The USAspending API enables programmatic tracking of federal grant allocations related to the opioid crisis:


Code
# Query USAspending for opioid-related grants
# Documentation: https://api.usaspending.gov/

usa_spending_url <- "https://api.usaspending.gov/api/v2/search/spending_by_award/"

# Dynamic date window: past 5 years from today
date_end   <- Sys.Date()
date_start <- as.Date(paste0(as.integer(format(date_end, "%Y")) - 5, "-01-01"))
year_end   <- as.integer(format(date_end, "%Y"))
year_start <- year_end - 5

# POST request body: opioid-related keyword search for grants
request_body <- list(
  filters = list(
    keywords = list("opioid", "overdose", "substance abuse"),
    time_period = list(
      list(start_date = format(date_start, "%Y-%m-%d"),
           end_date   = format(date_end, "%Y-%m-%d"))
    ),
    award_type_codes = c("02", "03", "04", "05")
  ),
  fields = c(
    "Award ID", "Recipient Name", "Award Amount",
    "Awarding Agency", "Awarding Sub Agency",
    "Start Date", "End Date", "Description",
    "generated_internal_id"
  ),
  limit = 100,
  page = 1
)

# Paginate to collect all available awards
# USAspending API caps at 100 results per page
all_results <- list()
for (pg in 1:100) {
  request_body$page <- pg
  spending_response <- request(usa_spending_url) |>
    req_body_json(request_body) |>
    req_perform()

  page_data <- spending_response |>
    resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE)

  if (is.null(page_data$results) || nrow(page_data$results) == 0) break
  all_results[[pg]] <- page_data$results
  if (nrow(page_data$results) < 100) break
}

spending_data <- page_data  # keep metadata from last response
spending_data$results <- bind_rows(all_results)

# Extract the results into a tidy dataframe
awards <- spending_data$results |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(
    award_amount = as.numeric(`Award Amount`),
    # Build a clickable link to the award page on USAspending.gov
    Award = ifelse(
      !is.na(generated_internal_id) & generated_internal_id != "",
      paste0('<a href="https://www.usaspending.gov/award/',
             generated_internal_id, '" target="_blank">', `Award ID`, '</a>'),
      `Award ID`
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(award_amount))

# Truncate descriptions to a readable length for the table
awards <- awards |>
  mutate(
    Description = ifelse(
      nchar(Description) > 120,
      paste0(substr(Description, 1, 120), "..."),
      Description
    )
  )

# Display the top 5 awards by amount
awards |>
  select(
    Award, `Recipient Name`, `Award Amount`,
    `Awarding Sub Agency`, Description, `Start Date`
  ) |>
  head(5) |>
  knitr::kable(
    format.args = list(big.mark = ","),
    caption = paste0("Top 5 Opioid-Related Federal Grant Awards by Amount (",
                     year_start, "-", year_end, ")")
  )

3.6.1.1 What Are These Awards Funding?
The word cloud below visualizes the most frequent terms appearing in award descriptions across all 10,000 retrieved opioid-related grants. Larger words appear more often, revealing the dominant themes in federal spending. Use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer to search, filter, and download the full award dataset.


Code
# Use the full (untruncated) descriptions from the API response
full_descriptions <- spending_data$results$Description

# Tokenize into individual words, remove stop words and generic terms
word_counts <- tibble(text = full_descriptions) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), text != "") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !word %in% c("award", "grant", "funding", "program", "provide",
                  "project", "based", "including", "support", "services",
                  "funds", "federal", "national", "department", "health",
                  "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "01", "02", "12",
                  as.character(year_start:year_end), "cfda", "fy"),
    nchar(word) > 2,
    !grepl("^[0-9]+$", word)
  ) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(word_counts |> head(80), aes(label = word, size = n, color = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(family = "sans", shape = "square") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 28) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#204d70", high = "#2494f7") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0)
  )











3.6.1.2 Federal Funding Over Time
The chart below plots individual grant award amounts by their start date, colored by awarding sub-agency. Hover over each point to see the recipient and award details.


Code
# Prepare data for the timeline chart
funding_timeline <- awards |>
  mutate(
    start_date = as.Date(`Start Date`),
    award_amount = as.numeric(`Award Amount`),
    agency = `Awarding Sub Agency`,
    recipient = `Recipient Name`,
    hover_text = paste0(
      "<b>", agency, "</b><br>",
      "Recipient: ", recipient, "<br>",
      "Award: $", formatC(award_amount, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(start_date), !is.na(award_amount))

# Brand-aligned color palette for agencies
agency_colors <- c(
  "#2494f7", "#00a4bb", "#01272f", "#204d70",
  "#e07b39", "#6b4c9a", "#d94f4f", "#2ca02c",
  "#8c564b", "#17becf", "#bcbd22", "#7f7f7f"
)

funding_plot <- plot_ly(
  data = funding_timeline,
  x = ~start_date,
  y = ~award_amount,
  color = ~agency,
  colors = agency_colors,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(size = 8, opacity = 0.7),
  text = ~hover_text,
  hoverinfo = "text"
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = paste0("Opioid-Related Federal Grant Awards Over Time (",
                    year_start, "-", year_end, ")"),
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Award Start Date",
      type = "date"
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Award Amount ($)",
      separatethousands = TRUE,
      type = "log"
    ),
    legend = list(
      title = list(text = "Awarding Sub Agency"),
      orientation = "h",
      y = -0.25,
      x = 0,
      font = list(size = 9)
    ),
    hovermode = "closest",
    margin = list(b = 120),
    height = 800
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

funding_plot








3.6.1.3 Total Federal Funding by Agency
The chart below summarizes annual opioid-related grant funding by awarding sub-agency. The stacked bars show each agency’s contribution, while the line traces the overall annual total.


Code
# Summarize annual funding by sub-agency
funding_by_agency <- awards |>
  mutate(
    start_date = as.Date(`Start Date`),
    start_year = as.integer(format(start_date, "%Y")),
    agency = `Awarding Sub Agency`
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(start_year), !is.na(award_amount)) |>
  group_by(start_year, agency) |>
  summarise(
    total = sum(award_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_awards = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Overall annual totals for the line trace
funding_annual_total <- funding_by_agency |>
  group_by(start_year) |>
  summarise(
    grand_total = sum(total, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_awards = sum(n_awards),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

agency_bar_plot <- plot_ly() |>
  add_trace(
    data = funding_by_agency,
    x = ~start_year,
    y = ~total,
    color = ~agency,
    colors = agency_colors,
    type = "bar",
    text = ~paste0(
      "<b>", agency, "</b><br>",
      "Year: ", start_year, "<br>",
      "Awards: ", n_awards, "<br>",
      "Total: $", formatC(total, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    ),
    hoverinfo = "text"
  ) |>
  add_trace(
    data = funding_annual_total,
    x = ~start_year,
    y = ~grand_total,
    type = "scatter",
    mode = "lines+markers",
    name = "Annual Total",
    line = list(color = "#020506", width = 3),
    marker = list(color = "#020506", size = 8),
    text = ~paste0(
      "<b>Annual Total</b><br>",
      "Year: ", start_year, "<br>",
      "Awards: ", n_awards, "<br>",
      "Total: $", formatC(grand_total, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    ),
    hoverinfo = "text"
  ) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = paste0("Total Opioid-Related Federal Grant Funding by Agency (",
                    year_start, "-", year_end, ")"),
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(title = "Year", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "Total Funding ($)", separatethousands = TRUE),
    barmode = "stack",
    legend = list(
      title = list(text = "Awarding Sub Agency"),
      orientation = "h",
      y = -0.25, x = 0,
      font = list(size = 9)
    ),
    hovermode = "x unified",
    margin = list(b = 120),
    height = 800
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

agency_bar_plot






For the full searchable and downloadable award dataset, use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer.






NoteData Source



USAspending is the official U.S. government open data source for federal spending. HHS TAGGS provides more granular transaction-level tracking of HHS grant obligations specifically.
crumbs: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis

title: 2  The U.S. Drug Overdose Crisis
text:
3.7 Research Literature and Databases
Beyond surveillance data, a large and growing body of peer-reviewed research examines the epidemiology, pharmacology, policy responses, and social determinants of the overdose crisis. Several public databases allow programmatic access to this literature and to information about funded research projects.

3.7.1 NIH RePORTER (Funded Research Projects)
NIH RePORTER tracks all NIH-funded research projects, including active grants, publications, patents, and clinical trials. Its API supports POST-based queries to identify what research is currently being funded on any topic.


Code
# NIH RePORTER API: find funded opioid overdose research projects
# Documentation: https://api.reporter.nih.gov/

reporter_url <- "https://api.reporter.nih.gov/v2/projects/search"

# Paginate to collect all available projects
all_projects <- list()
for (pg in 0:99) {
  reporter_body <- list(
    criteria = list(
      advanced_text_search = list(
        operator = "and",
        search_field = "projecttitle,terms",
        search_text = "opioid overdose fentanyl"
      ),
      fiscal_years = as.list((year_end - 4L):year_end),
      exclude_subprojects = TRUE
    ),
    offset = pg * 100,
    limit = 100,
    sort_field = "FiscalYear",
    sort_order = "desc"
  )

  reporter_response <- request(reporter_url) |>
    req_body_json(reporter_body) |>
    req_perform()

  reporter_data <- reporter_response |>
    resp_body_json()

  if (length(reporter_data$results) == 0) break
  all_projects[[pg + 1]] <- reporter_data$results
  if (length(reporter_data$results) < 100) break
}

reporter_total <- reporter_data$meta$total

# Parse all results into a dataframe
projects <- lapply(unlist(all_projects, recursive = FALSE), function(p) {
  tibble(
    project_num   = p$project_num %||% NA_character_,
    fiscal_year   = p$fiscal_year %||% NA_integer_,
    title         = p$project_title %||% NA_character_,
    pi_name       = {
      if (!is.null(p$principal_investigators) && length(p$principal_investigators) > 0)
        p$principal_investigators[[1]]$full_name
      else NA_character_
    },
    organization  = p$organization$org_name %||% NA_character_,
    ic_name       = {
      ics <- p$agency_ic_fundings
      if (!is.null(ics) && length(ics) > 0) ics[[1]]$abbreviation %||% NA_character_
      else NA_character_
    },
    award_amount  = p$award_amount %||% NA_real_,
    abstract_text = p$abstract_text %||% NA_character_
  )
}) |>
  bind_rows()


The search matched 815 projects. 815 were retrieved, representing $608,979,046 in total funding.

3.7.1.1 NIH Project Awards Over Time
The chart below plots individual NIH-funded project award amounts by fiscal year, colored by NIH Institute/Center (IC). Hover over each point to see the project title, PI, and award amount.


Code
library(plotly)

nih_scatter_df <- projects |>
  filter(!is.na(award_amount), !is.na(fiscal_year)) |>
  mutate(
    ic_label = ifelse(is.na(ic_name), "Other", ic_name),
    hover_text = paste0(
      "<b>", ic_label, "</b><br>",
      "PI: ", pi_name, "<br>",
      "Title: ", substr(title, 1, 80), "<br>",
      "Award: $", formatC(award_amount, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    )
  )

ic_colors <- c(
  "#2494f7", "#00a4bb", "#01272f", "#204d70",
  "#e07b39", "#6b4c9a", "#d94f4f", "#2ca02c",
  "#8c564b", "#17becf", "#bcbd22", "#7f7f7f"
)

nih_scatter_plot <- plot_ly(
  data = nih_scatter_df,
  x = ~fiscal_year,
  y = ~award_amount,
  color = ~ic_label,
  colors = ic_colors,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers",
  marker = list(size = 8, opacity = 0.7),
  text = ~hover_text,
  hoverinfo = "text"
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = paste0("NIH Opioid Research Project Awards (",
                    year_end - 4, "-", year_end, ")"),
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(title = "Fiscal Year", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Award Amount ($)",
      separatethousands = TRUE,
      type = "log"
    ),
    legend = list(
      title = list(text = "NIH Institute/Center"),
      orientation = "h",
      y = -0.25, x = 0,
      font = list(size = 9)
    ),
    hovermode = "closest",
    margin = list(b = 120),
    height = 800
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

nih_scatter_plot








3.7.1.2 NIH Funding by Institute/Center
The stacked bar chart below summarizes annual NIH opioid research funding by Institute/Center. The line traces the overall annual total.


Code
# Summarize funding by fiscal year and IC
nih_by_ic <- projects |>
  filter(!is.na(award_amount), !is.na(fiscal_year)) |>
  mutate(ic_label = ifelse(is.na(ic_name), "Other", ic_name)) |>
  group_by(fiscal_year, ic_label) |>
  summarise(
    total = sum(award_amount, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_projects = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

nih_annual_total <- nih_by_ic |>
  group_by(fiscal_year) |>
  summarise(
    grand_total = sum(total, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_projects = sum(n_projects),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

nih_timeline_plot <- plot_ly() |>
  add_trace(
    data = nih_by_ic,
    x = ~fiscal_year,
    y = ~total,
    color = ~ic_label,
    colors = ic_colors,
    type = "bar",
    text = ~paste0(
      "<b>", ic_label, "</b><br>",
      "FY ", fiscal_year, "<br>",
      "Projects: ", n_projects, "<br>",
      "Total: $", formatC(total, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    ),
    hoverinfo = "text"
  ) |>
  add_trace(
    data = nih_annual_total,
    x = ~fiscal_year,
    y = ~grand_total,
    type = "scatter",
    mode = "lines+markers",
    name = "Annual Total",
    line = list(color = "#020506", width = 3),
    marker = list(color = "#020506", size = 8),
    text = ~paste0(
      "<b>Annual Total</b><br>",
      "FY ", fiscal_year, "<br>",
      "Projects: ", n_projects, "<br>",
      "Total: $", formatC(grand_total, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
    ),
    hoverinfo = "text"
  ) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "NIH Opioid Overdose Research Funding by Fiscal Year and IC",
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(title = "Fiscal Year", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "Total Award Amount ($)", separatethousands = TRUE),
    barmode = "stack",
    legend = list(
      title = list(text = "NIH Institute/Center"),
      orientation = "h",
      y = -0.25, x = 0,
      font = list(size = 9)
    ),
    hovermode = "x unified",
    margin = list(b = 120),
    height = 800
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

nih_timeline_plot








3.7.1.3 What Is NIH Funding?
The word cloud below visualizes the most frequent terms appearing in project abstracts across all 815 retrieved NIH-funded opioid research projects, revealing the dominant research themes. Use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer to search, filter, and download the full project dataset.


Code
# Tokenize abstracts into individual words, remove stop words and generic terms
nih_word_counts <- tibble(text = projects$abstract_text) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), text != "") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !word %in% c("study", "research", "project", "aim", "aims",
                  "specific", "propose", "proposed", "will", "use",
                  "data", "based", "including", "provide", "develop",
                  "support", "national", "health", "program", "grant",
                  "funding", "abstract", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5",
                  "r01", "r21", "r34", "k01", "k23", "u01", "p50"),
    nchar(word) > 2,
    !grepl("^[0-9]+$", word)
  ) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(nih_word_counts |> head(80), aes(label = word, size = n, color = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(family = "sans", shape = "square") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 28) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#204d70", high = "#2494f7") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0)
  )

The table below shows the five largest NIH-funded opioid overdose research projects by award amount. For the full searchable and downloadable project dataset, use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer.


Code

  arrange(desc(award_amount)) |>
  head(5) |>
  select(
    `Project Number` = project_num,
    `Fiscal Year` = fiscal_year,

    Organization = organization,
    `Award Amount` = award_amount

    caption = paste0(
      "Top 5 NIH-Funded Opioid Overdose Research Projects by Award Amount (",
      year_end - 4, "-", year_end, ")"
    )
  )

NoteData Source



NIH RePORTER is the authoritative source for federally funded biomedical research. It covers grants from NIH, CDC, FDA, AHRQ, SAMHSA, and other HHS agencies. No authentication is required. The API returns project details, funding amounts, and links to resulting publications.





3.7.2 ClinicalTrials.gov (Clinical Research)
ClinicalTrials.gov registers clinical studies on a wide range of conditions, including opioid use disorder treatment trials, naloxone distribution studies, and harm reduction interventions. The v2 API supports structured queries.


Code
# ClinicalTrials.gov v2 API: find opioid-related clinical trials
# Documentation: https://clinicaltrials.gov/data-api/about-api
# Paginate using nextPageToken to collect up to ~200 trials

ct_url <- "https://clinicaltrials.gov/api/v2/studies"

all_trials_raw <- list()
next_token <- NULL
for (pg in 1:10) {
  ct_query <- list(
    `query.cond`  = "opioid overdose",
    `query.intr`  = "fentanyl OR naloxone OR buprenorphine",
    `filter.overallStatus` = "RECRUITING,ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING,COMPLETED",
    countTotal    = "true",
    pageSize      = 100,
    sort          = "LastUpdatePostDate:desc"
  )
  if (!is.null(next_token)) ct_query$pageToken <- next_token

  ct_response <- request(ct_url) |>
    req_url_query(!!!ct_query) |>
    req_perform()

  ct_data <- ct_response |>
    resp_body_json()

  if (length(ct_data$studies) == 0) break
  all_trials_raw[[pg]] <- ct_data$studies
  next_token <- ct_data$nextPageToken
  if (is.null(next_token)) break
}

ct_total <- ct_data$totalCount

trials <- lapply(unlist(all_trials_raw, recursive = FALSE), function(s) {
  proto <- s$protocolSection
  id_mod <- proto$identificationModule
  status_mod <- proto$statusModule
  sponsor_mod <- proto$sponsorCollaboratorsModule
  design_mod <- proto$designModule
  desc_mod <- proto$descriptionModule

  tibble(
    nct_id      = id_mod$nctId %||% NA_character_,
    title       = id_mod$briefTitle %||% NA_character_,
    status      = status_mod$overallStatus %||% NA_character_,
    start_date  = status_mod$startDateStruct$date %||% NA_character_,
    sponsor     = sponsor_mod$leadSponsor$name %||% NA_character_,
    enrollment  = design_mod$enrollmentInfo$count %||% NA_integer_,
    description = desc_mod$briefSummary %||% NA_character_
  )
}) |>
  bind_rows()


The search matched 66 studies. 66 trials were retrieved for analysis.

3.7.2.1 Clinical Trials Over Time
The chart below shows the number of opioid-related clinical trials by their start year, broken down by status.


Code
library(plotly)

trials_timeline <- trials |>
  mutate(
    start_year = as.integer(substr(start_date, 1, 4))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(start_year)) |>
  count(start_year, status, name = "n_trials") |>
  arrange(start_year)

# Brand-aligned status colors
status_colors <- c(
  "COMPLETED" = "#2494f7",
  "ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING" = "#00a4bb",
  "RECRUITING" = "#204d70"
)

ct_timeline_plot <- plot_ly(
  data = trials_timeline,
  x = ~start_year,
  y = ~n_trials,
  color = ~status,
  colors = status_colors,
  type = "bar",
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", start_year, "</b><br>",
    status, ": ", n_trials, " trials"
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Opioid-Related Clinical Trials by Start Year and Status",
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(title = "Start Year", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "Number of Trials"),
    barmode = "stack",
    legend = list(
      title = list(text = "Trial Status"),
      orientation = "h",
      y = -0.2, x = 0
    ),
    hovermode = "x unified"
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

ct_timeline_plot








3.7.2.2 Clinical Trial Research Themes
The word cloud below visualizes the most frequent terms in trial titles and descriptions, revealing the dominant intervention and population themes in clinical research. Use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer to search, filter, and download the full trial dataset.


Code
ct_word_counts <- trials |>
  mutate(text = paste(title, description, sep = " ")) |>
  select(text) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), text != " ") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !word %in% c("study", "trial", "clinical", "phase",
                  "participants", "purpose", "determine",
                  "evaluate", "assess", "including", "based",
                  "aims", "aim", "objective", "primary"),
    nchar(word) > 2,
    !grepl("^[0-9]+$", word)
  ) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(ct_word_counts |> head(80), aes(label = word, size = n, color = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(family = "sans", shape = "square") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 28) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#204d70", high = "#2494f7") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0)
  )

TipReproducibility



ClinicalTrials.gov does not require authentication. The v2 API returns JSON by default and supports filtering by condition, intervention, status, location, and date range. This is useful for mapping the research landscape around specific interventions (e.g., naloxone distribution, buprenorphine access).





3.7.3 PubMed (NCBI E-utilities API)
PubMed indexes over 37 million biomedical citations. The NCBI E-utilities API enables programmatic search and retrieval without authentication (subject to rate limits of 3 requests per second without an API key, or 10 per second with one).


Code
library(httr2)
library(jsonlite)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# NCBI E-utilities: search PubMed for recent opioid overdose research
# Documentation: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK25501/

# Step 1: Search for article IDs matching our query
esearch_url <- "https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esearch.fcgi"

search_response <- request(esearch_url) |>
  req_url_query(
    db       = "pubmed",
    term     = "opioid overdose fentanyl United States[Title/Abstract]",
    retmax   = 10000,
    sort     = "date",
    retmode  = "json",
    mindate  = format(date_start, "%Y/%m/%d"),
    maxdate  = format(date_end, "%Y/%m/%d"),
    datetype = "pdat"
  ) |>
  req_perform()

search_results <- search_response |>
  resp_body_json()

# Extract PubMed IDs
pmids <- search_results$esearchresult$idlist

# Step 2: Fetch article summaries for those IDs
esummary_url <- "https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esummary.fcgi"

if (length(pmids) > 0) {
  summary_response <- request(esummary_url) |>
    req_url_query(
      db      = "pubmed",
      id      = paste(pmids, collapse = ","),
      retmode = "json"
    ) |>
    req_perform()

  summaries <- summary_response |>
    resp_body_json()

  # Parse into a tidy dataframe
  articles <- lapply(pmids, function(id) {
    article <- summaries$result[[id]]
    tibble(
      pmid      = id,
      title     = article$title %||% NA_character_,
      journal   = article$fulljournalname %||% NA_character_,
      pub_date  = article$pubdate %||% NA_character_,
      doi       = {
        doi_ids <- Filter(function(x) x$idtype == "doi", article$articleids)
        if (length(doi_ids) > 0) doi_ids[[1]]$value else NA_character_
      }
    )
  }) |>
    bind_rows()
}


The search matched 317 articles. 317 were retrieved for analysis.

3.7.3.1 Publication Trends Over Time
The chart below shows the volume of opioid overdose fentanyl research published over time. Each point represents the number of articles published in a given month.


Code
library(plotly)
library(lubridate)

# Parse publication dates into proper Date objects
# PubMed dates come in formats like "2025 Jan 15", "2025 Mar", "2025"
pubmed_timeline <- articles |>
  mutate(
    pub_date_parsed = case_when(
      grepl("^\\d{4} \\w+ \\d+", pub_date) ~
        as.Date(pub_date, format = "%Y %b %d"),
      grepl("^\\d{4} \\w+", pub_date) ~
        as.Date(paste0(pub_date, " 01"), format = "%Y %b %d"),
      grepl("^\\d{4}$", pub_date) ~
        as.Date(paste0(pub_date, " Jan 01"), format = "%Y %b %d"),
      TRUE ~ NA_Date_
    ),
    pub_month = floor_date(pub_date_parsed, "month")
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(pub_month))

# Count articles per month
monthly_counts <- pubmed_timeline |>
  count(pub_month, name = "n_articles") |>
  arrange(pub_month)

# Interactive time series
pubmed_plot <- plot_ly(
  data = monthly_counts,
  x = ~pub_month,
  y = ~n_articles,
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "lines+markers",
  line = list(color = "#2494f7", width = 2),
  marker = list(color = "#2494f7", size = 6),
  hovertemplate = paste(
    "<b>%{x|%B %Y}</b><br>",
    "Articles: %{y}<extra></extra>"
  )
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Opioid Overdose Fentanyl Research: Publications per Month",
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Publication Month",
      type = "date",
      rangeslider = list(visible = TRUE)
    ),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "Number of Articles"
    ),
    hovermode = "x unified"
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

pubmed_plot








3.7.3.2 Research Themes in Article Titles
The word cloud below visualizes the most frequent terms appearing in the titles of all 317 retrieved PubMed articles. This provides a quick thematic overview of the research landscape. Use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer to search, filter, and download the full publication dataset.


Code
# Tokenize article titles into individual words
pubmed_title_words <- tibble(text = articles$title) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), text != "") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !word %in% c("study", "analysis", "among", "associated",
                  "united", "states", "results", "based",
                  "findings", "using", "related", "data"),
    nchar(word) > 2,
    !grepl("^[0-9]+$", word)
  ) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(pubmed_title_words |> head(80), aes(label = word, size = n, color = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(family = "sans", shape = "square") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 28) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#204d70", high = "#2494f7") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0)
  )











3.7.3.3 Most Recent Publications
The table below shows the five most recently published opioid overdose fentanyl articles indexed in PubMed. For the full searchable and downloadable publication dataset, use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer.


Code
articles |>
  arrange(desc(pub_date)) |>
  head(5) |>
  select(
    PMID = pmid, Title = title, Journal = journal,
    `Publication Date` = pub_date
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = paste0(
      "5 Most Recent PubMed Articles: Opioid Overdose Fentanyl Research (",
      format(date_start, "%Y"), "-", format(date_end, "%Y"), ")"
    )
  )



5 Most Recent PubMed Articles: Opioid Overdose Fentanyl Research (2021-2026)

TipReproducibility



For higher rate limits, register for a free NCBI API key at ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/account and append &api_key=YOUR_KEY to requests. The E-utilities support complex Boolean queries using MeSH terms (e.g., "Opiate Overdose"[MeSH] AND "Fentanyl"[MeSH]) for more precise results.





3.7.4 Europe PMC (Open Access Literature)
Europe PMC provides open access to a worldwide collection of life science publications, including all PubMed content plus preprints, patents, and clinical guidelines. Its REST API is particularly useful for retrieving full-text open access articles.


Code
# Paginate to collect all available results (pageSize max is 1000)

epmc_url <- "https://www.ebi.ac.uk/europepmc/webservices/rest/search"

epmc_all <- list()
for (pg in 1:50) {
  epmc_response <- request(epmc_url) |>
    req_url_query(
      query      = paste0("(opioid overdose fentanyl) AND (PUB_YEAR:[",
                          year_start, " TO ", year_end, "])"),
      format     = "json",
      pageSize   = 1000,
      cursorMark = if (pg == 1) "*" else epmc_cursor,
      resultType = "core"
    ) |>
    req_perform()

  epmc_data <- epmc_response |>
    resp_body_json()

  if (length(epmc_data$resultList$result) == 0) break
  epmc_all[[pg]] <- epmc_data$resultList$result
  epmc_cursor <- epmc_data$nextCursorMark %||% NULL
  if (is.null(epmc_cursor) || length(epmc_data$resultList$result) < 1000) break
}

epmc_hit_count <- epmc_data$hitCount

epmc_articles <- lapply(unlist(epmc_all, recursive = FALSE), function(a) {
  tibble(
    pmid            = a$pmid %||% NA_character_,
    title           = a$title %||% NA_character_,
    journal         = a$journalTitle %||% NA_character_,
    year            = a$pubYear %||% NA_character_,
    first_pub_date  = a$firstPublicationDate %||% NA_character_,
    is_open_access  = a$isOpenAccess %||% "N",
    cited_by_count  = a$citedByCount %||% 0L,
    doi             = a$doi %||% NA_character_,
    abstract        = a$abstractText %||% NA_character_
  )
}) |>
  bind_rows()


The search matched 7,105 results. 7,105 articles were retrieved, of which 4509 are open access.

3.7.4.1 Europe PMC: Publication Timeline
The chart below shows the volume of opioid overdose fentanyl research indexed in Europe PMC by publication year.


Code
library(plotly)

epmc_by_year <- epmc_articles |>
  filter(!is.na(.data$year)) |>
  mutate(pub_year = as.integer(.data$year)) |>
  count(pub_year, name = "n_articles") |>
  arrange(pub_year)

epmc_timeline_plot <- plot_ly(
  data = epmc_by_year,
  x = ~pub_year,
  y = ~n_articles,
  type = "bar",
  marker = list(color = "#00a4bb"),
  text = ~paste0("<b>", pub_year, "</b><br>Articles: ", n_articles),
  hoverinfo = "text"
) |>
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Europe PMC: Opioid Overdose Fentanyl Publications by Year",
      font = list(size = 16)
    ),
    xaxis = list(title = "Publication Year", dtick = 1),
    yaxis = list(title = "Number of Articles"),
    hovermode = "x unified"
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = TRUE)

epmc_timeline_plot








3.7.4.2 Europe PMC: Research Themes
The word cloud below visualizes the most frequent terms in article titles and abstracts retrieved from Europe PMC. Use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer to search, filter, and download the full article dataset.


Code
epmc_word_counts <- epmc_articles |>
  mutate(text = paste(title, abstract, sep = " ")) |>
  select(text) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), text != " ") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(
    !word %in% c("study", "results", "background", "methods",
                  "conclusions", "objective", "purpose", "findings",
                  "data", "based", "including", "conclusion",
                  "aim", "abstract", "introduction"),
    nchar(word) > 2,
    !grepl("^[0-9]+$", word)
  ) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(epmc_word_counts |> head(80), aes(label = word, size = n, color = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(family = "sans", shape = "square") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 28) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#204d70", high = "#2494f7") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0)
  )











3.7.4.3 Most-Cited Articles
The table below shows the five most-cited opioid overdose fentanyl articles indexed in Europe PMC. For the full searchable and downloadable article dataset, use the Drug Overdose Crisis Explorer.


Code
epmc_articles |>
  mutate(
    `Open Access` = ifelse(is_open_access == "Y", "Yes", "No"),
    `Cited By` = as.integer(cited_by_count)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(`Cited By`)) |>
  head(5) |>
  select(
    PMID = pmid, Title = title, Journal = journal,
    Year = year, `Open Access`, `Cited By`
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(
    caption = paste0(
      "Top 5 Most-Cited Articles: Opioid Overdose Fentanyl Research (",
      year_start, "-", year_end, ")"
    )
  )

title: 3  Development Tools
text:
4.1 Tools Used



Tool
Purpose




Quarto
Reproducible document and book generation


R / RStudio
Data analysis, visualization, and Shiny dashboards


Python
API access, data engineering, PDF extraction


VS Code
Primary editor for development


GitHub
Version control and publishing (GitHub Pages)


GitHub Copilot
AI-assisted development


Mermaid
Text-based diagrams for data flows and frameworks


Tableau Public
Interactive dashboard publishing

title: 3  Development Tools
text:
4.2 Local Development
To build this book locally:
# Render the book
quarto render

# Preview with live reload
quarto preview

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5 Glossary
Key terms used throughout this book, organized alphabetically.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.1 C

CDC WONDER

Wide-ranging ONline Data for Epidemiologic Research. The primary federal system for querying mortality and other public health data from the National Vital Statistics System. Accessible at wonder.cdc.gov.

ClinicalTrials.gov

A database of privately and publicly funded clinical studies conducted around the world. Maintained by the National Library of Medicine (NLM), it registers trials on conditions including opioid use disorder treatments and harm reduction interventions. Accessible via a public v2 API at clinicaltrials.gov.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.2 D

DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)

A composite measure of disease burden combining years of life lost (YLLs) due to premature mortality with years lived with disability (YLDs). One DALY represents one lost year of healthy life.

Data Sovereignty

The principle that data is subject to the laws and governance of the nation or community from which it originates. Particularly relevant for Indigenous data, where tribal nations assert authority over data collection, ownership, and use.

Data Use Agreement (DUA)

A formal agreement governing the terms under which restricted datasets may be accessed and used, specifying permitted uses, security requirements, and reporting obligations.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.3 E

E-utilities (NCBI Entrez Programming Utilities)

A set of server-side programs providing a stable interface to the Entrez query and database system at the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI). Used for programmatic access to PubMed and other NCBI databases. Documentation at ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK25501.

Europe PMC

An open science platform that provides access to a worldwide collection of life science publications, including all PubMed content plus preprints, patents, and clinical guidelines. Offers a REST API for programmatic search and retrieval at europepmc.org.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.4 F

Fentanyl

A synthetic opioid 50 to 100 times more potent than morphine. Illicitly manufactured fentanyl is the primary driver of drug overdose deaths in the United States since approximately 2013.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.5 G

Global Burden of Disease (GBD)

A comprehensive epidemiological study managed by the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) that provides estimates for over 375 diseases and injuries across 204 countries.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.6 H

Health Expenditure

The total spending on health by all financing agents (government, private, out-of-pocket, external aid) within a jurisdiction.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.7 I

ICD-10

The International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision. The standard diagnostic coding system used worldwide for mortality and morbidity classification.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.8 M

MOUD (Medications for Opioid Use Disorder)

Evidence-based pharmacological treatments for opioid use disorder, including buprenorphine, methadone, and naltrexone. Previously referred to as medication-assisted treatment (MAT).

Morbidity

The state of having a disease or medical condition. Measured through incidence (new cases) and prevalence (existing cases) rates.

Mortality Rate

The frequency of death in a defined population over a specified time period, typically expressed per 100,000 population.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.9 N

Naloxone

An opioid antagonist medication that rapidly reverses the effects of opioid overdose. Available as a nasal spray (Narcan) or injectable formulation. A core component of harm reduction strategies.

NCD (Non-Communicable Disease)

Chronic diseases not transmitted from person to person, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, and chronic respiratory disease.

NIH RePORTER

Research Portfolio Online Reporting Tools. A searchable database of federally funded biomedical research projects maintained by the National Institutes of Health. Covers grants from NIH, CDC, FDA, AHRQ, SAMHSA, and other HHS agencies. Accessible via a public API at reporter.nih.gov.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.10 O

OData

Open Data Protocol. A REST-based data access protocol used by WHO Global Health Observatory and other data portals for programmatic querying.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.11 P

PubMed

A free search engine maintained by the National Library of Medicine (NLM) that indexes over 37 million citations and abstracts of biomedical literature. The primary resource for searching peer-reviewed public health and medical research. Accessible at pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.12 S

Socrata

A data platform used by many U.S. state and local governments to host open data portals with built-in API access.

SUDORS (State Unintentional Drug Overdose Reporting System)

A CDC surveillance system that collects detailed toxicological and death scene information on unintentional and undetermined-intent drug overdose deaths, providing more substance-specific detail than death certificates alone.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.13 V

VSRR (Vital Statistics Rapid Release)

A CDC program that publishes provisional mortality data, including monthly drug overdose death counts, before finalized annual data are available.

title: 4  Glossary
text:
5.14 Y

YLD (Years Lived with Disability)

The number of years lived in less than ideal health, weighted by the severity of the disability.

YLL (Years of Life Lost)

The number of years of potential life lost due to premature death, calculated from a reference life expectancy.
